introSection: Batman The Escape was a steel stand-up roller coaster previously located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA. The ride previously operated as Shockwave at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California, USA and at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA.

sections.title: Six Flags Magic Mountain (1986-1988)
sections.content: On May 16, 1986, Shockwave opened at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California. The ride was located on the spot where Sarajevo Bobsleds stood for the previous two seasons, which was relocated to Six Flags Over Texas. It was painted entirely black. The motto for the ride, featured in commercials, was "If you think you can take it... Stand up." Due to the Six Flags ride rotation program, like Sarajevo Bobsleds and several other Six Flags coasters at the time, Shockwave operated briefly, lasting three seasons before being dismantled in 1988 and sent to another Six Flags park. Shockwave was replaced by Psyclone in 1991 which closed in 2006, which in turn was replaced by Apocalypse in 2009. A new stand-up coaster named Riddler's Revenge opened in 1998, ten years after the closure of Shockwave.

sections.title: Six Flags Great Adventure (1990-1992)
sections.content:
In 1989, land clearing began at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey and towards the end of summer season, parts began arriving. Meanwhile, a competition was held asking guests to "Stand Up and Name It", with the winning contestant receiving a $250 premium bond, a family season pass for 1990 and an invitation for the ride's media day. In the end, the roller coaster was called Shockwave, the same name it operated under at Magic Mountain.

Shockwave had a press preview on April 20, 1990 and opened to the public the following day. It was painted blue with white supports. Shockwave suffered many mechanical issues and was so loud that the noise could be heard throughout the area. After operating for three seasons, it was dismantled in 1992 with the plot left vacant. It was once again relocated to another Six Flags park. The queue line and walkways remained unused until the openings of Dare Devil Dive in 1997 and Houdini's Great Escape in 1999. In 2011, another stand-up roller coaster, Green Lantern, opened at Six Flags Great Adventure, replacing Great American Scream Machine.

sections.title: Six Flags AstroWorld (1993-2005)
sections.content:
In December 1992, it was announced that Shockwave would be relocated to Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas. The attraction opened there on April 24, 1993. It was painted entirely white and called Batman The Escape.

The ride's queue line had immersive theming, which included the Batmobile, a Batcave area, an Arctic Park area and a crashed police car. The Batcave itself was previously built as part of the former Alpine Sleigh Ride attraction which operated from 1968 to 1983 and was later converted into a walk-through attraction as part of the Enchanted Kingdom children's area as Discovery Mountain closing in 1992. This was the second Batman/DC coaster to open at a Six Flags park.

The side bars were removed from the trains in 1994, with the last car of each train being removed to create a third train in 1999.

The Batcave and Batmobile theming of the queue line were removed and closed off in 1998. This was also in addition due to the ride getting unpopular due to its roughness.

In 2002, the ride's queue line was spruced up with new landscaping and the Batcave was given new lighting effects.

In 2003, Batman The Escape's loop was repainted.

In 2004, it was painted with yellow track and black supports.

The ride continued to operate until the entire theme park was shut down the following year on October 30, 2005 and was subsequently dismantled.

sections.title: Six Flags Darien Lake (2006-2017)
sections.content: During the 2005-2006 off season, Six Flags sent and placed the roller coaster at Six Flags Darien Lake in Darien Center, New York, which Six Flags owned at the time. However, shortly after, Six Flags announced plans to sell Darien Lake due to their financial problems, and Darien Lake was acquired by PARC Management in 2007, who subsquently gained the ride from Six Flags who left it behind with them. The ride remained in storage outside in a field opposite the park. In May 2017, the general manager at the time said there were no plans to reopen the roller coaster and it would be scrapped in the future. Not long after by the end of the year, the parts were eventually removed from Darien Lake during December 2017/January 2018 and the ride was scrapped.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride had a curved first drop which led immediately into the tall vertical loop, the only inversion on the ride. This was followed by a twist reminiscent of an inclined loop and a rise into the mid-course brake run. The second half of the ride had more twists and turns, with the track passing through the center of the vertical loop. Cobra at La Ronde opened in 1995 and was a clone of this ride. It was removed from the park's website in 2017 before being demolished and scrapped in March 2018. Of the three stand-up roller coasters Intamin built, only Shockwave at Drayton Manor still operates, now converted to a sit-down coaster known as The Wave since 2024.

sections.title: Theme
sections.content: Shockwave did not have a theme when it operated at Magic Mountain and Great Adventure. While at AstroWorld, the roller coaster was based on Gotham City. Guests journeyed through Arctic Park and Gotham City Cold Storage before boarding the roller coaster to make their escape. With Batman Returns out at the time, the villain in this ride was The Penguin.

sections.title: External links
sections.content:
* Batman The Escape before its 2004 repaint


 * Batman The Escape over Looney Tunes Town

fullText:
Batman The Escape was a steel stand-up roller coaster previously located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA. The ride previously operated as Shockwave at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California, USA and at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA.



On May 16, 1986, Shockwave opened at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California. The ride was located on the spot where Sarajevo Bobsleds stood for the previous two seasons, which was relocated to Six Flags Over Texas. It was painted entirely black. The motto for the ride, featured in commercials, was "If you think you can take it... Stand up." Due to the Six Flags ride rotation program, like Sarajevo Bobsleds and several other Six Flags coasters at the time, Shockwave operated briefly, lasting three seasons before being dismantled in 1988 and sent to another Six Flags park. Shockwave was replaced by Psyclone in 1991 which closed in 2006, which in turn was replaced by Apocalypse in 2009. A new stand-up coaster named Riddler's Revenge opened in 1998, ten years after the closure of Shockwave.

In 1989, land clearing began at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey and towards the end of summer season, parts began arriving. Meanwhile, a competition was held asking guests to "Stand Up and Name It", with the winning contestant receiving a $250 premium bond, a family season pass for 1990 and an invitation for the ride's media day. In the end, the roller coaster was called Shockwave, the same name it operated under at Magic Mountain.

Shockwave had a press preview on April 20, 1990 and opened to the public the following day. It was painted blue with white supports. Shockwave suffered many mechanical issues and was so loud that the noise could be heard throughout the area. After operating for three seasons, it was dismantled in 1992 with the plot left vacant. It was once again relocated to another Six Flags park. The queue line and walkways remained unused until the openings of Dare Devil Dive in 1997 and Houdini's Great Escape in 1999. In 2011, another stand-up roller coaster, Green Lantern, opened at Six Flags Great Adventure, replacing Great American Scream Machine.

In December 1992, it was announced that Shockwave would be relocated to Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas. The attraction opened there on April 24, 1993. It was painted entirely white and called Batman The Escape.

The ride's queue line had immersive theming, which included the Batmobile, a Batcave area, an Arctic Park area and a crashed police car. The Batcave itself was previously built as part of the former Alpine Sleigh Ride attraction which operated from 1968 to 1983 and was later converted into a walk-through attraction as part of the Enchanted Kingdom children's area as Discovery Mountain closing in 1992. This was the second Batman/DC coaster to open at a Six Flags park.

The side bars were removed from the trains in 1994, with the last car of each train being removed to create a third train in 1999.

The Batcave and Batmobile theming of the queue line were removed and closed off in 1998. This was also in addition due to the ride getting unpopular due to its roughness.

In 2002, the ride's queue line was spruced up with new landscaping and the Batcave was given new lighting effects.

In 2003, Batman The Escape's loop was repainted.

In 2004, it was painted with yellow track and black supports.

The ride continued to operate until the entire theme park was shut down the following year on October 30, 2005 and was subsequently dismantled.

During the 2005-2006 off season, Six Flags sent and placed the roller coaster at Six Flags Darien Lake in Darien Center, New York, which Six Flags owned at the time. However, shortly after, Six Flags announced plans to sell Darien Lake due to their financial problems, and Darien Lake was acquired by PARC Management in 2007, who subsquently gained the ride from Six Flags who left it behind with them. The ride remained in storage outside in a field opposite the park. In May 2017, the general manager at the time said there were no plans to reopen the roller coaster and it would be scrapped in the future. Not long after by the end of the year, the parts were eventually removed from Darien Lake during December 2017/January 2018 and the ride was scrapped.

The ride had a curved first drop which led immediately into the tall vertical loop, the only inversion on the ride. This was followed by a twist reminiscent of an inclined loop and a rise into the mid-course brake run. The second half of the ride had more twists and turns, with the track passing through the center of the vertical loop. Cobra at La Ronde opened in 1995 and was a clone of this ride. It was removed from the park's website in 2017 before being demolished and scrapped in March 2018. Of the three stand-up roller coasters Intamin built, only Shockwave at Drayton Manor still operates, now converted to a sit-down coaster known as The Wave since 2024.

Shockwave did not have a theme when it operated at Magic Mountain and Great Adventure. While at AstroWorld, the roller coaster was based on Gotham City. Guests journeyed through Arctic Park and Gotham City Cold Storage before boarding the roller coaster to make their escape. With Batman Returns out at the time, the villain in this ride was The Penguin.
